markdown.headingText: Point Python to your source folder
markdown.markdown:
```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| cache: false
import sys
import os

sys.path.append(os.getcwd())

from src.data_factory import generate_nhs_dummy_data
from src.models import get_dashboard_forecast_outputs

# Generate data and model once
ts_df, _ = generate_nhs_dummy_data()

# Run Forecast Model
forecast_fig, forecast_metrics = get_dashboard_forecast_outputs()
```

## Force-wide Absence Forecast

This chart compares the performance of a sophisticated SARIMA model against a simple Seasonal Naive forecast. The SARIMA model provides a more accurate prediction by capturing complex trends and seasonality in the historical absence data.

```{python}
#| label: forecast-plot
#| output: asis
# Force pure HTML rendering to prevent Jupyter from falling back to a raw JSON text dump
print(forecast_fig.to_html(full_html=False, include_plotlyjs='cdn'))
```

## Model Performance & Interpretation

```{python}
#| output: asis
mae = forecast_metrics['arima_mae']
naive_mae = forecast_metrics['naive_mae']
pct_better = ((naive_mae - mae) / naive_mae) * 100

print(f"**Forecasting Accuracy (Mean Absolute Error):** The model is currently able to predict the absence rate with an average error of **{mae:.1f}**. \n")
print(f"This is **{pct_better:.1f}% more accurate** than simply assuming the absence rate will be exactly the same as it was at this time last year (the baseline 'Seasonal Naive' method, which has an average error of **{naive_mae:.1f}**).\n\n")

print("> *Note: A 'Concordance Score' is used to measure the accuracy of Time-to-Event (Survival) models. For time series forecasting like this chart, we use Mean Absolute Error (MAE), which tells us exactly how much our forecast is off by on average.*")
```

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| cache: false
import sys
import os

# Point Python to your source folder
sys.path.append(os.getcwd())

from src.data_factory import generate_nhs_dummy_data
from src.models import get_dashboard_forecast_outputs

# Generate data and model once
ts_df, _ = generate_nhs_dummy_data()

# Run Forecast Model
forecast_fig, forecast_metrics = get_dashboard_forecast_outputs()
```

## Force-wide Absence Forecast

This chart compares the performance of a sophisticated SARIMA model against a simple Seasonal Naive forecast. The SARIMA model provides a more accurate prediction by capturing complex trends and seasonality in the historical absence data.

```{python}
#| label: forecast-plot
#| output: asis
# Force pure HTML rendering to prevent Jupyter from falling back to a raw JSON text dump
print(forecast_fig.to_html(full_html=False, include_plotlyjs='cdn'))
```

## Model Performance & Interpretation

```{python}
#| output: asis
mae = forecast_metrics['arima_mae']
naive_mae = forecast_metrics['naive_mae']
pct_better = ((naive_mae - mae) / naive_mae) * 100

print(f"**Forecasting Accuracy (Mean Absolute Error):** The model is currently able to predict the absence rate with an average error of **{mae:.1f}**. \n")
print(f"This is **{pct_better:.1f}% more accurate** than simply assuming the absence rate will be exactly the same as it was at this time last year (the baseline 'Seasonal Naive' method, which has an average error of **{naive_mae:.1f}**).\n\n")

print("> *Note: A 'Concordance Score' is used to measure the accuracy of Time-to-Event (Survival) models. For time series forecasting like this chart, we use Mean Absolute Error (MAE), which tells us exactly how much our forecast is off by on average.*")
```
